title: Pauli-structured preconditioning for quantum linear system solvers
authors: Hantao Nie, Zhijian Lai, Dong An
abstract: Preconditioning is a fundamental technique for accelerating classical linear system solvers, and understanding when its benefits persist in quantum linear system (QLS) solvers is important for assessing the practical resource requirements of quantum linear algebra. In QLS algorithms, however, the potential advantage of preconditioning may be offset by the normalization overhead incurred by composing separate block-encodings of the system matrix and the preconditioner, as observed in recent work. This limitation leaves open whether additional algebraic structure can make preconditioning effective in quantum access models. Motivated by this question, we show that Pauli-structured representations of both the system matrix and the preconditioner allow the preconditioned operator to be accessed through regrouped Pauli expansions. In this setting, algebraic regrouping of Pauli products can reduce the Pauli coefficient weight of the preconditioned operator, thereby altering the normalization parameters relevant to quantum algorithms. We derive explicit size and coefficient-weight bounds for the regrouped Pauli representations, and we trace their consequences for both direct block-encoding constructions and randomized Pauli linear system solvers. These results identify when Pauli-structured preconditioning can reduce the effective complexity parameters of QLS algorithms, rather than merely improving the classical condition number. Numerical experiments on a finite-dimensional synthetic benchmark show reductions in norm-aware direct block-encoding diagnostics and in the randomized QLS per-sample depth proxy.
summary: This paper investigates the feasibility of using preconditioning in quantum linear system solvers by analyzing the underlying algebraic structure using Pauli expansions. It demonstrates that by exploiting Pauli-structured representations, the overhead associated with composing the preconditioned operator can be reduced. This confirms that Pauli-structured preconditioning can lead to genuine improvements in the complexity of randomized QLS algorithms.
main_problem: The paper addresses whether preconditioning techniques can genuinely improve the complexity of Quantum Linear System (QLS) solvers, given that normalization overheads can negate classical condition number improvements.
main_result: Pauli-structured representations allow for a regrouping of Pauli products that can reduce the effective Pauli coefficient weight of the preconditioned operator, leading to genuine complexity reductions in the randomized QLS solver model.
method: The authors analyze the QLS problem using Pauli-structured representations, comparing the complexity derived from separate block-encoding composition versus direct block-encoding of the preconditioned operator.
model_or_system: The model is the Quantum Linear System (QLS) problem, $Ax=b$, where the system matrix $A$ and preconditioner $P$ are assumed to admit Pauli expansions. The analysis considers both coherent block-encoding and randomized Pauli access models.
key_observables: Pauli coefficient weight, effective QLS condition parameter ($\kappa_{	ext{eff}}$), and the randomized QLS per-sample depth proxy ($DRQLS$).
important_parameters: The Pauli coefficient weight ($w(\cdot)$), the minimum singular value ($\sigma_{\min}(\cdot)$), and the normalization factors ($\alpha_A, \alpha_P$).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes that the system matrix $A$ and preconditioner $P$ can be accurately represented by Pauli expansions, and it distinguishes between the limitations of separate block-encoding versus direct encoding.
figures_summary: Table I summarizes the performance comparison for a synthetic benchmark, showing the ratio $R_n$ (preconditioned/unpreconditioned depth proxy) and direct block-encoding diagnostics for the $A_n$ family.
paper_structure: The paper first establishes the limitation of separate block-encoding composition (Theorem 3), then introduces the Pauli-structured approach, deriving bounds on Pauli coefficient weights for the preconditioned operator, and finally applying this to the randomized QLS solver to show genuine complexity improvements.
why_interesting: It provides a rigorous theoretical framework for determining when structural algebraic properties (Pauli structure) can overcome fundamental overheads (normalization/synthesis) that limit the practical quantum advantage in solving linear systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
